source: WHO + WSO + Mayo Clinic Clinical Guide

chunks.text: A stroke occurs when the blood supply to part of the brain is interrupted or reduced, depriving brain tissue of oxygen and causing cell death within minutes.
One in four adults over age 25 will have a stroke in their lifetime. It is a leading cause of long-term disability and the second leading cause of death globally.
Common signs follow the FAST acronym: Facial drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, and Time to call emergency services. Sudden severe headaches and vision loss are also frequent.
Ischemic strokes (87% of cases) are caused by blood clots, while hemorrhagic strokes result from ruptured vessels. Major risk factors include untreated hypertension and atrial fibrillation.
Immediate diagnosis is performed via CT or MRI scans to differentiate between clot-based and bleed-based strokes, which determines the emergency treatment path.
Ischemic strokes are treated with thrombolytic drugs (clot-busters) or mechanical thrombectomy. Hemorrhagic strokes focus on controlling bleeding and reducing intracranial pressure.
Managing high blood pressure is the single most effective way to prevent stroke. Other measures include anticoagulation for heart rhythm issues and smoking cessation.
